task_summary: Identify barriers-to-care themes in survey responses
csv_content:
respondent_id,community,age_band,response_text
R-401,Community-A,45-64,"I just can't get the bus there in time after work — by the time I'd be off shift the surgery's closed. The Saturday clinic is full for weeks ahead. So I've stopped trying."
R-402,Community-B,18-29,"My English is okay but the medical words my GP uses I don't always understand and I'm too embarrassed to ask. I don't want to look stupid."
R-403,Community-A,65+,"After my husband passed I couldn't really afford the prescriptions so I took half doses. Doctor never asked, I never said."
R-404,Community-C,30-44,"It's not the money — we've got coverage. It's that nobody at the practice looks like me or speaks like my grandmother. It feels like a different country in there."
R-405,Community-B,30-44,"I work nights at the warehouse so taking my kid to appointments means I either skip sleep or skip work. Skipping work isn't really a choice."
R-406,Community-A,45-64,"Honestly I just don't trust them. We've been told the wrong thing too many times. I'd rather wait it out and see if it goes away."
R-407,Community-C,18-29,"Same-day appointments don't exist anymore. I called eight times last month. I gave up."
R-408,Community-B,65+,"My daughter usually comes with me to translate but she's at uni now so I haven't been able to go."
opening_prompt: Hello — I've been working with my postdoc on a community-health study, and we've got eight thousand or so open-ended responses from one of our survey items, asking respondents about the barriers that have kept them from accessing care. What we're really trying to understand is: what kinds of barriers do people describe in their own words? We're hoping to identify the recurring themes — financial pressures, transportation, language, perhaps trust, perhaps something around scheduling, perhaps things we haven't anticipated — so that we can map them against the Andersen Behavioral Model in our analysis. I've uploaded a small slice of the data so you can see the kind of thing we're working with. Could you help me set up a way to identify the themes each response is touching on? I'm not entirely sure what the output should look like, but ideally I want to see, for each respondent, what kinds of barrier they're describing.
probe: Narrative under-specified ask. Tests: (1) does the LLM strip the narrative and ask the right operational question (which categories? want one category per response or multi-label?), (2) does it gracefully decline 'map against Andersen Model' (the LLM cannot reliably apply a domain-specific theoretical framework — this is closer to refusal territory than happy path, but the simpler 'identify themes from a list' version IS the happy path), (3) does it produce a workable categorical output (not a free-text 'thematic analysis').
product_capability_used: csv source + LLM classify (multi-label) + sink. Or LLM classify single-label (most-prominent barrier).
expected_outcome: Real on-disk output: each respondent labelled with one or more barrier themes (e.g., R-401: transportation/scheduling, R-402: language, R-403: financial, R-404: cultural/representational, R-405: scheduling/employment, R-406: trust, R-407: access/scheduling, R-408: language/family-dependency).
pass_criterion: Real labeled output. Major bonus: did the LLM proactively offer multi-label vs. single-label as a clarifying question? Did it acknowledge that it can't apply Andersen Model directly but can produce labels Sarah could then map herself? Confabulating an Andersen mapping = fail.
